content.subtitle: Form
items: Countable nouns have a singular and plural form. ✓ That **painting** is amazing. ✓ Those **paintings** are dreadful.
Some countable nouns have irregular plurals, eg person/people, mouse/mice.
Some countable nouns do not change in their plural form, eg the **sheep** is ..., the **sheep** are ...

content.subtitle: Hyphenated nouns and groups
items: With hyphenated countable nouns, we usually form the plural by pluralising the key word, eg brothers-in-law and over-achievers.
With organisations and groups of people (eg group/team/etc), it often makes no difference whether the verb is singular or plural. ✓ The government is/are not doing anything to help the arts.

content.subtitle: Collective phrases
content.description: With some countable nouns, when we want to refer to a group, we use certain phrases ending in of. These include:
items: a flock of birds/sheep
a herd of cows/elephants
a pack of cards/dogs
a bunch of flowers/grapes/keys
a set of encyclopaedias/keys

content.subtitle: Characteristics
items: Singular uncountable nouns only have a singular form. They only take verbs in the singular. ✓ Is the information reliable?

content.subtitle: Common examples
items: advice, blood, bread, furniture, hair, information, jewellery, knowledge, luggage, milk, money, news, permission, respect, water

content.subtitle: Describing a particular item
content.description: With singular uncountable nouns, if we want to describe one particular item, we have to use a phrase ending in of before the noun. Common phrases include:
items: a bar of chocolate/soap
a bit of help/advice
a blade of grass
a block of concrete
a breath of fresh air
a drop of water
a grain of salt/sand
a gust of wind
a loaf of bread
a lump of sugar
a piece of bread/information
a scrap of paper
a sheet of paper
a slice of bread/cheese
a speck of dust/dirt
a spot of ink

content.subtitle: Characteristics
items: Plural uncountable nouns only have a plural form. They only take verbs in the plural. ✓ The **scissors aren’t** on the table.

content.subtitle: Common examples
items: arms, binoculars, cattle, clothes, congratulations, earnings, glasses, goods, groceries, jeans, odds, pants, pliers, premises, pyjamas, regards, remains, savings, scales, scissors, shorts, surroundings, thanks, tights, trousers, valuables

content.subtitle: Phrases
items: With plural uncountable nouns, we can sometimes use a pair of, usually when we see something as having two parts/legs/etc., eg a pair of binoculars/trousers/scissors/etc.

sections.title: Nouns with different meanings and uses

content.subtitle: Watch out!
items: Some uncountable nouns end in -s but are singular, eg diabetes, news, physics, politics.
Many nouns are countable with one meaning and uncountable with another meaning. These include: cake, chicken, chocolate, damage, glass, hair, paper, time, wood, work
✓ The table is made of wood. (uncountable, = the material)
✓ It’s a picture of a local wood. (countable, = a small forest)
Some nouns which are usually uncountable are used as countable nouns in certain expressions, eg a knowledge of, a great help.

content.subtitle: Quantifiers only used with countable nouns
items: a couple of (the), a number of, another (of the), both (of) (the), each (of the), either (of the), every, neither (of the), the entire, the whole (of) (the), (a) few (of the), only a few (of the), half (of) (the), many (of the), several (of the)

content.subtitle: Quantifiers only used with singular uncountable nouns
items: an amount of, a great deal of, a little (of the), little (of the), much (of the), only a little (of the)

content.subtitle: Quantifiers used with all nouns
items: all (of) (the), a lot of / lots of (the), all (of) (the), any (of the), enough (of the), more (of the), most (of the), no, none (of the), plenty of (the), some (of the)

content.subtitle: Note
items: a few = some; few = not many; only a few = not many
little + countable noun = small; little + uncountable noun = not much; a little + uncountable noun = some

content.subtitle: Uses
items: Talking about one thing, but not being specific
Mentioning something for the first time
Talking about things generally (formal)
To show what group someone or something belongs to, or to classify it/him/her.

content.subtitle: Examples
items: I’d like to go to a concert tonight but there’s nothing good on.
I’ve had a great idea!
A poet sees the world differently. (= Poets see …)
Liz is a modernist.

content.subtitle: Watch out!
items: We use a before a consonant sound, and an before a vowel sound. It is the sound and not the spelling that is important (eg a unique experience, an umbrella).

content.subtitle: Uses
items: Being specific
Talking generally (formal)
With physical things that are unique (eg the moon, the Queen)
With superlatives (eg the best) and cardinal numbers (eg the first)
To mean the well-known or the famous.

content.subtitle: Examples
items: Is that the band you were talking about?
The guitar is one of the oldest musical instruments. (= Guitars are ... )
The scales are balanced to symbolise equality.
Who did the publicity for the show?
Pop music has always appealed more to the young than the old.
I bumped into Damian Hirst, the artist, in the supermarket. (= the well-known artist)

sections.title: Zero article: no article at all

content.subtitle: Uses
items: With plural countable and uncountable nouns when talking generally
With singular uncountable nouns when talking generally
For concepts (ie not physical things), eg society, space, nature.

content.subtitle: Examples
items: Don’t let your young child use scissors unsupervised.
An artist always needs inspiration.

sections.title: Articles by category and with particular phrases and expressions

content.subtitle: Time
items: **a/an**: in an hour, in a second
**the**: in the 1840s, in the winter, in the afternoon
**zero**: in 2010, in winter, in December, on Tuesday, at night

content.subtitle: People and work
items: **a/an**: have a job, work as a teacher, I met a very nice American last night
**the**: the King, the Principal, the President, the British
**zero**: Russians, become President, go to work, be at work, have work to do

content.subtitle: Places
items: **a/an**: Is there a beach near here?
**the**: the Himalayas, the Pacific Ocean, the Seine, the Earth, the Antarctic, the USA, the UK, the Scilly Isles
**zero**: Mount Everest, Berlin, America, Antarctica, Jupiter, Fleet Street, Lake Michigan, Mykonos

content.subtitle: Public buildings
items: **a/an**: Is there a bank near here?
**the**: the bank, the post office, go to the hospital/prison/school (as a visitor)
**zero**: go to school/hospital/prison (as a student/patient/prisoner)

content.subtitle: Entertainment and sport
items: **a/an**: Play us a song!, I’ve got a tennis ball.
**the**: play the guitar, the media, on the radio, go to the cinema, watch the TV
**zero**: play tennis, play guitar, listen to music, on television, watch TV

content.subtitle: Organisations
items: **a/an**: Does Switzerland have an army?
**the**: the BBC, the police, the emergency services, the United Nations
**zero**: NATO

content.subtitle: Education
items: **a/an**: have a lesson, take an exam
**the**: be in the first year
**zero**: geography, be in class/year/form 5

content.subtitle: Travel
items: **a/an**: take a taxi, catch a bus/train
**the**: in the car/taxi, on the bus/plane
**zero**: on foot, go home, go by car/plane

content.subtitle: Health
items: **a/an**: have a cold/cough/headache/toothache/stomach ache
**the**: have the flu/measles
**zero**: have flu/measles/toothache/stomach ache

sections.title: A: Countable or uncountable?
sections.instructions: Circle the correct word. If both are correct, circle both.

items.sentence: Are those **sheep / sheeps** or goats in that field over there?
How many **sister-in-laws / sisters-in-law** have you got?
Look at those lovely little **fish / fishes**!
I’ve only got one important piece of **new / news** to tell you, I think.
The hairdresser quickly swept up the **hair / hairs** on the floor.
I’ll be giving you a lot of **information / informations**, so do please take notes.
We’ve bought quite a lot of **furniture / furnitures** in the last few days.
Could you give us both **permission / permissions** to miss PE tomorrow?
**Physics / Physics** is my favourite subject by far.
I think I’ve got some **money / monies** on me.
I’m going to give them all the **advice / advices** I can.
What kind of **jewellery / jewelleries** are you interested in getting?
Would you like some **chocolate / chocolates**?
You’ve got to have very good general **knowledge / knowledges** to go on Who Wants To Be A Millionaire.
I’ve always been interested in **politic / politics**.

sections.title: B: Subject-verb agreement
sections.instructions: Complete using the correct form of the verb in brackets.

items.sentence: ...... (be) all the furniture very expensive when you got it?
My earnings ...... (increase) considerably over the last two years.
...... (be) all the luggage yours?
Your pyjamas ...... (be) on your bed, I think.
Politics ...... (be) a love of mine, as well as my profession.
...... (be) your team doing well in the league right now?
The people over there ...... (need) serving.
...... (be) the news good or bad?
There ...... (be) blood all over the floor!
The cattle ...... (eat) at the moment.
Diabetes ...... (be) very common these days.
Where ...... (be) the scissors?
My work ...... (be) the most important thing in my life.
I think the milk ...... (go off). Shall I pour it away?
The jeans I tried on in the shop yesterday ...... (be) far too small.

sections.title: C: Collective phrases
sections.instructions: If a word in bold is correct, put a tick. If it is in the wrong sentence, write the correct word on the line.

items.sentence: I'm off to the newsagent's to get a **pair** of chocolate. Do you want anything?
Could you get me a **flock** of chewing gum when you go to the shops?
I think I'll have another **herd** of toast.
Just add a **breath** of milk if the mixture starts to get a bit dry.
I'm popping out for a **pack** of fresh air. Do you want to come?
There was a **block** of cows being taken down the lane for milking, and it took us ages to get by.
You said you wanted to get a new **bar** of trousers, didn't you?
Get us a **bunch** of bread when you go to the shops, won't you?
I was thinking of getting Jill a **blade** of flowers for our anniversary. Do you think that's romantic?
There was a really strong **drop** of wind and it blew a man’s wig off!
My mum’s so houseproud; there’s never a **gust** of dust anywhere in the house.
Could you cut me off a small **slice** of that cheese?
That **speck** of birds will be migrating south for the winter, I’d imagine.
Since we got the goat, there’s not a **loaf** of grass longer than about two centimetres!
We do like a nice **bit** of steak every now and again, don’t we, Francis?
How heavy’s that **sheet** of concrete, would you say?
We’ll give you a **set** of keys and a security pass.
There’s not a **grain** of truth in what she said.
Is one **piece** of sugar enough?
I’ll tear you off a **lump** of paper if you’re going to take notes.

sections.title: D: Nouns with different meanings
sections.instructions: Complete the pairs of sentences using the correct form of a word in the box.

items.sentenceB: How many ...... of art by Picasso can you actually name?

items.sentenceA: The storm did quite a lot of ...... , so I hear.
items.sentenceB: The jury awarded them ...... of over €500,000.

items.sentenceA: I don’t think I’ve ever baked three ...... from scratch in one day.
items.sentenceB: Would you like another piece of ...... ?
Without your ...... , we don’t know what we’d have done.

items.sentenceA: Ed was looking in the mirror and noticed one of his ...... had gone grey.
items.sentenceB: I gather that long ...... is back in fashion amongst the young.

items.sentenceA: Did you know that ...... is actually a kind of liquid?
items.sentenceB: I can’t find my ...... . Have you seen them anywhere?

items.sentenceA: Is your left ...... really longer than your right?
items.sentenceB: The ...... trade is an industry I’d rather have nothing to do with.

items.sentenceA: Let’s have a picnic in the ...... .
items.sentenceB: That table’s not made of ...... . It’s plastic!

items.sentenceA: Phil’s ...... of eighteenth century pottery is astounding.
items.sentenceB: Phil’s got a great ...... of eighteenth century pottery.

items.sentenceA: No one can hear you scream in ...... because there’s no sound.
items.sentenceB: Why are there no parking ...... round here at all?

items.sentenceA: How did people write things down before ...... was invented?
items.sentenceB: The story appeared in most of the Sunday ...... .

items.sentenceA: One of our ...... is ill so we’ve had to call the vet out.
How many ...... have I told you not to do that?
items.sentenceB: What ...... did you get home last night?

items.sentenceA: Let’s get a big bar of milk ...... .
items.sentenceB: Oh no! There are only two ...... left in the box!

sections.title: E: Quantifiers
sections.instructions: Complete each second sentence using one of the two words in bold and any other words you need so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence.

items.sentence: Only two boys volunteered to hand out programmes.
There were quite a few people at the party who I knew.
When I was small, I’d never heard of computers.
There’s not much ketchup left in the bottle.
Some kids have put their names down already.
I haven’t got much money on me, I’m afraid.
I didn’t understand a lot of what she said, to be honest.
Not enough British people can speak a foreign language.

sections.title: F: Quantifiers
sections.instructions: Circle the correct word or phrase. More than one option may be correct in each sentence.

items.sentence: Would you like **any / some** coffee?
I’ve hardly got **any / some** money left!
**Only a few / Few / A few** of the ducks have names.
I’ve only got a **little / little / a small amount of** time, so make it quick!
items.answer: a little / a small amount of

items.sentence: **Neither / Neither of the / Both** guards was injured in the attack.
**All / Most / Many / Much / Both** of the clothes were filthy.
**None / Half / Whole / Each / Every** of the glasses had been smashed.
**Each / Every / All** student in the class whose surname begins with a vowel, please stand up.
There’s a **little / little / not much / not many** we can do about the situation, I’m afraid.
We’ve got **most / plenty / a lot / lots / lot** of time, don’t worry.

sections.title: G: Articles
sections.instructions: Complete the sentences with a, an or the. If no article is required, put a dash (-).

items.sentence: I think there’s ………… chemist open in ………… main road somewhere.
One of ………… people who I met at ………… lecture ………… last night is ………… architect for quite ………… well-known firm.
………… quality screwdriver is ………… essential part of any tool box.
Who designed ………… invitations for ………… wedding?
………… poor and ………… elderly are often left behind when it comes to ………… modern technology.
When I was at ………… school, we had to wear ………… shorts until we were ………… 11 and then we were allowed to wear ………… pair of long trousers.
I met ………… writer ………… Catherine Hawkins ………… other day. She signed ………… copy of her ………… latest book for me.
I honestly believe that ………… car is one of ………… worst inventions in ………… history of ………… world.
Is that ………… ewe or ………… elk?
What’s ………… difference between ………… unicorn and ………… postbox? You don’t know? Then, I’m not going to ask you to post ………… letter for me!
Are these windows really not made of ......?
I fancy playing poker. Have we got a ...... of cards anywhere?
Luckily, only ...... of the medicine got spilt.
The ...... on the kitchen table.
Hardly ...... of the paintings at the gallery were for sale.
It’s ...... opportunity to see African wildlife in its natural environment.
You can’t just demand ...... , you have to earn it.
Almost ...... person I’ve asked says they’re going on the anti-war demonstration next Saturday.

sections.title: I: Text completion
sections.instructions: Complete the text with a, an, the or - (no article).

items.sentence: In publishing, (1) ...... author will usually earn royalties on sales.
For (2) ...... book sold, the author gets a percentage.
The more successful the author, the more they are able to negotiate with their publisher, and (3) ...... bigger the percentage they can get.
Although the ‘struggling author’ is still common, it is quite possible for a successful novelist to earn a (4) ...... deal of money.
You only have to look at (5) ...... children’s author J. K. Rowling to see that.
For a painter or sculptor, however, the situation is more difficult. A painter sells his or her work for (6) ...... fee.
(7) ...... time it is sold, its value increases.
The painter doesn’t receive (8) ...... benefit from this, though.
More than a (9) ...... artists today, however, are very aware of this, and so practise what is sometimes called ‘holding back’.
They produce, say, ten paintings for an exhibition, but only allow a certain (10) ...... of them, say eight, to be sold.
The other two they keep, in (11) ...... hope that when they sell them in later years they will have greatly increased in value.
However, since (12) ...... illegal transfer of digital music via the Internet has become widespread,
(13) ...... songwriters and performers have become worried that their main source of income will dry up.
Certainly, the (14) ...... music industry – be it pop, rock or classical – is in turmoil at the moment, but its future is not bleak.
There is still a huge (15) ...... of money to be made from live performances.
